serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of apple juice is one glass of approximately 250 ml (250 g), commonly consumed as a breakfast beverage or snack drink.

nutrient_source.title: Apple Juice — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010154 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record. USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 09016, Apple juice, canned or bottled, unsweetened) was consulted as a secondary cross-reference to confirm plausibility of values; HPB values are used as primary.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Apple juice GI = 44 (±6) is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 44 × 12.75 / 100 = 5.6 (serving = 250 g, carbohydrate = 5.1 g/100 g × 250 g = 12.75 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Free fructose without dietary fibre; sodium 13 mg/100 g; SSB-equivalent classification
hypertension.guidance: Apple juice contains 4.8 g free fructose per 100 g with zero dietary fibre, meaning fructose is absorbed rapidly and may drive insulin-mediated sodium retention; AHA guidelines classify fruit juices as SSB-equivalents and recommend replacing juice with whole fruit to benefit from fibre-mediated fructose modulation and to reduce cardiovascular risk associated with liquid sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Liquid fructose (4.8 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre; rapid glycaemic response despite moderate GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Apple juice delivers concentrated liquid fructose (4.8 g/100 g) with no dietary fibre to slow absorption, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; ADA guidelines classify fruit juices as unsuitable for people with type 2 diabetes and recommend consuming whole apples instead, where intact fibre substantially attenuates the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar liquid with no fibre; regular consumption linked to elevated triglycerides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of fruit juice as a free-sugar liquid beverage is associated with elevated serum triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk; the American Heart Association recommends limiting fruit juice to no more than 125 ml per day and preferring whole fruit, which provides fibre, polyphenols, and a lower glycaemic impact.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No anti-inflammatory nutrients; concentrated fructose without fibre or polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Apple juice provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory components (no omega-3, polyphenols, whole-food fibre, or antioxidants at therapeutic levels); EULAR and Arthritis Foundation dietary guidance recommends whole fruit over juice to obtain quercetin and fibre from apple skin, which have demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apple juice (苹果汁, Jus Epal) is a clear to pale-golden beverage produced by pressing or extracting liquid from fresh apples, then filtering to remove pulp and fibre. It is widely consumed across Singapore and the broader Chinese-speaking world as a breakfast drink, snack beverage, or mixer. The flavour is mildly sweet and lightly tart, with a thin, watery body and no significant mouthfeel from fibre or pulp. Per 100 g, apple juice provides 22 kcal, 5.1 g carbohydrate (of which 4.8 g is free sugar, predominantly fructose), 0.1 g fat, and 0 g protein. Dietary fibre is absent because pressing and filtration remove the apple's cell-wall matrix. Sodium is low at 13 mg/100 g. Potassium (51 mg), calcium (3 mg), magnesium (5 mg), and iron (0.2 mg) are present in small amounts. The glycaemic index is 44 (low), but the absence of fibre means fructose is absorbed rapidly, making the glycaemic load clinically meaningful at scale. Apple juice is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage equivalent by ADA and AHA guidelines due to its liquid fructose content without fibre modulation. It is unsuitable for people with type 2 diabetes and should be consumed with caution by those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Whole apples are consistently preferred over juice in clinical dietary guidance because intact fibre slows fructose absorption and provides polyphenols (quercetin) with anti-inflammatory properties. Apple juice is vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is apple juice good for high blood pressure?
Apple juice vs whole apple nutrition

confidence_reason: Input record is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are from this canonical approved primary source. GI value of 44 is confirmed in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008). No estimation or analogy was required for core nutrients.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo; apple juice is a filtered fruit beverage containing no protein by formulation — confirmed zero. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo; filtration and pressing remove all fibre from the apple — confirmed zero. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central (NDB 09016). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data not reported in HPB Foodo for this beverage entry; USDA FoodData Central reports negligible values but HPB is the primary source and does not disaggregate fat fractions. water_g set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo source record. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, B-vitamins, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo source record for this entry; USDA FoodData Central reports small amounts of vitamin C (~0.9 mg/100g) but HPB is the primary source and does not report this value — follow-up enrichment from USDA recommended. The input health_suitability entries for diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid_arthritis have been preserved and expanded into the health_context section with standardised clinical language. The condition key 'diabetes' from the input has been mapped to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. Cultural tags from input (Chinese, SG) are preserved. Portions array was empty in input; a standard 250 ml glass serving has been assigned based on common Singapore beverage serving norms.

Added chinese_traditional '蘋果汁', malay 'Jus Epal', tamil 'ஆப்பிள் சாறு', hindi 'सेब का रस' to local_names to satisfy G6 Singaporean multilingual requirement.
